coredata.dc:description: The high-value conversion of waste resources to carbon materials offers an opportunity to alleviate the resource crisis. Owing to their low cost and easy preservation, iron-based nanoenzymes are receiving increasing attention in the fields of catalysis, detection and biomedicine. In this work, for the first time, paper mill sludge (PMS), which contains iron ions from a flocculant used in paper mill wastewater treatment, is used as a precursor to produce iron-doped carbon quantum dots (Fe-CQDs) by oil bath pyrolysis process. The synthesized Fe-CQDs with carboxyl groups and abundant carbon-deficient structure, exhibit higher peroxidase-like activity. Compared to horseradish peroxidase (HRP), Fe-CQDs have a better affinity for the substrate and better stability. Based on the good affinity of Fe-CQDs for H2O2, a rapid colourimetric assay for ascorbic acid (AA) detection was developed with a wide range of 1–120 μM and a detection limit of 0.435 μM (3σ/k). The peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CQDs has been proved to originate from their ability to catalyze H2O2 to produce·OH, and successfully applied to determine AA content in the roots and leaves of sweet potato. This work indicates the reliability of the method in practical application and provides a greening strategy for the value-added utilization of solid sludge.

head.ce:title: Autogenous iron-based peroxidase-like nanozyme from paper mill sludge for ascorbic acid detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The high-value conversion of waste resources to carbon materials offers an opportunity to alleviate the resource crisis. Owing to their low cost and easy preservation, iron-based nanoenzymes are receiving increasing attention in the fields of catalysis, detection and biomedicine. In this work, for the first time, paper mill sludge (PMS), which contains iron ions from a flocculant used in paper mill wastewater treatment, is used as a precursor to produce iron-doped carbon quantum dots (Fe-CQDs) by oil bath pyrolysis process. The synthesized Fe-CQDs with carboxyl groups and abundant carbon-deficient structure, exhibit higher peroxidase-like activity. Compared to horseradish peroxidase (HRP), Fe-CQDs have a better affinity for the substrate and better stability. Based on the good affinity of Fe-CQDs for H2O2, a rapid colourimetric assay for ascorbic acid (AA) detection was developed with a wide range of 1–120 μM and a detection limit of 0.435 μM (3σ/k). The peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CQDs has been proved to originate from their ability to catalyze H2O2 to produce·OH, and successfully applied to determine AA content in the roots and leaves of sweet potato. This work indicates the reliability of the method in practical application and provides a greening strategy for the value-added utilization of solid sludge.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Paper mill sludge (PMS), a common solid waste, comes from pulp and paper wastewater after treatment [1]. Sludge treatment methods such as dumping, landfill and agricultural use have brought challenges with the rapid development of industrialization, and it is urgent to realize its resource utilization [2,3]. Converting sludge into carbon material for reuse is considered a promising way of environmental treatment [4] . PMS contains a rich source of carbon (cellulose, lignin and other organic matter) [5], as well as inorganic elements such as Fe, Al and Ca and their compounds [6]. Fe was mainly derived from FeCl3 which is commonly used as the flocculating regulator in sludge dewatering [7]. The rich composition and high inorganic content makes PMS an ideal precursor for the production of new carbon materials. In 2019, we successfully utilized PMS with melamine to prepare the iron, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon nanotube/nanoporous carbon nanocomposite (PMS-CNT/C) with a good ORR performance by pyrolysis [8]. Afterwards, a unique N-doped carbon nanotube/activated carbon (N-CNT/AC) framework was further prepared with PMS and melamine in 2020 for chromium (VI) reduction in microbial fuel cells [9]. In 2021, Zhang et al. also pyrolyzed PMS to obtain an iron-modified biochar for efficient adsorption of phosphate in water [10]. In the above studies, C-rich fractions were cleaved and structurally reorganised to achieve better catalytic/adsorption performance with high temperature. This suggests that the pyrolysis conversion of complex fractions of waste into high performance carbon materials is an effective resource conversion method, and promises to expand its application areas. However, these conversion methods involve high temperature processes (usually above 500°C) and unavoidable tail gas emissions, and there is an urgent need to develop safer and more effective methods for the thermal treatment of waste.

The peroxidase (POD) activity of Fe3O4 nanoparticles was first reported in 2007 [11]. Since then, iron-based nanozymes have been attracting worldwide attention and widely used in pharmaceutical analysis, biotechnology and environmental science [12,13]. A prosperously growing iron-based nanozyme family and the synthesis of enhanced catalytic activity have emerged as new hotspots [14,15]. Various methods have been explored to improve the catalytic activity of iron-based nanomaterials, including morphology control [16,17], elemental doping [18,19] and metal nanoparticle decoration [20], etc. Fe2+/Fe3+, which can disperse on the surface of iron-based nanoenzymes, acts as active centres to induce the redistribution of electrons on the catalyst surface to drive the Fenton/Fenton reaction or electron transfer process [21,22]. However, the preparation of these materials still relies on additional metal reagents and involves complex synthetic processes, posing challenges for regulated production. Additionally, finding sufficient carriers to activate the metal sites for Fe atom distribution is also essential to improving the catalytic activity of iron-based nanoenzymes [21,23].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with ultra-small particle size (below 10 nm) and ultra-high specific surface area have attracted much attention [16,18]. As previously reported, CQDs have a hydrophilic, highly stable and carbon-rich core [24,25]. Besides, the surface of CQDs is rich in functional groups such as -COOH, -OH and -NH2, which may not only be conducive to riveting active metal sites [24,26], but may also provide additional substrate binding sites to assist in electron transfer[27,28] . Based on these properties, many metal-doped CQDs such as iron-doped CQDs (Fe-CQDs)[29], Ce-doped CQDs [30], Mo/S co-doped CQDs [31] and CQDs metal composites such as CQDs/Fe3O4 hybrid nanofibers [32] and CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 [33] have been developed. Surprisingly, CQDs derived directly from biomass such as mustard seeds [34], leaf extracts of neem [35], also have POD-like properties without extra metal addition.The POD-like activity is supposed to arise from the formation of C and N rich defective structures after pyrolysis of complex precursors of biomass [36]. The hydrothermal method, one common method for CQD synthesis, is a pyrolysis method with the advantages of a controlled reaction, mild reaction and low energy consumption [25,37]. By controlling the reaction conditions, the hydrolysis of the precursor and the structural reformation process of the carbon core are regulated to achieve an efficient conversion of the precursor.

In this work, in view of the elemental composition of PMS and the superiority of pyrolytically derived CQD, the PMS, which contains iron originating from the flocculant of FeCl3 in paper mill wastewater treatment, is designed to prepare Fe-CQDs by a convenient and sustainable oil bath pyrolysis. The characterization results show that the Fe-CQDs have a C-rich defective core and abundant functional groups such as -COOH, -OH on the surface. Based on the H2O2-TMB sensing system, the Fe-CQDs is used for the colorimetric detection of H2O2 and ascorbic acid (AA), and further for the determination of AA content in roots, stems and leaves of sweet potato plants ( Scheme 1). Compared with most iron-based peroxidase mimics reported before, the developed synthetic strategy is environmentally friendly and economical, providing a practical approach for resource utilization of PMS.

The PMS was purchased from Dongguan Haolong Paper Co., Ltd. (Dongguan, China). Hydrogen peroxide (30% H2O2) and sodium acetate were obtained from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Acetic acid, KCl, NaCl, NH4Cl, MgCl2·6 H2O, Al(NO3)3·9 H2O, CaCl2, CuCl2·2 H2O, MnSO4·H2O, CdCl2·6 H2O, Pb(CH3COO)2·3 H2O, FeSO4·7 H2O, FeCl3·6 H2O and EDTA were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). ZnSO4·7 H2O, AgNO3, ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glc), D-fructose, glycine (Gly), citric acid (CA), salicylic acid (SA), glutathione (GSH), L-cysteine (L-Cys), DL-malic acid were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China). And, 3,3’,5,5’-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), saccharose, and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. (China). All chemicals were directly used without further purification.

Fe-CQDs were synthesized by a simple oil bath pyrolysis. Briefly, the PMS was washed with a dilute hydrochloric acid solution to remove excess ash. After that, 5.0 g of dried filter residue and 60 mL of deionized water were transferred to a round-bottom flask and heated to 200℃ for 12 h in an oil bath. After cooling, the solution was filtered and the filtrate was centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 15 min. The resulting Fe-CQDs solution was lyophilized and stored in a refrigerator at 4°C. The CQDs, which were prepared hydrothermally in a high pressure reaction kettle under 200℃ for 12 h were named as Hyd-CQDs for comparison.

The morphology of the Fe-CQDs was obtained by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20, USA). The crystalline phases of the samples were determined by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Germany). Zeta potentials was determined by a Zetasizer (NANO ZS, Malvern, Britain). Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected using a spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet iS 50 R, USA) with the samples loaded with KBr. The Raman spectrum was measured using a 632 nm laser on a Renishaw inVia-reflex spectroscopy. Absorption spectra were obtained with ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Lambda 25, USA). The atomic surface chemical states of the samples were obtained by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo, ESCALAB 250Xi, USA). The pH value of the solution was measured by the pH-meter (Clean Leau PH 550, China).

Peroxidase mimetic activity was measured by evaluating catalytic oxidation of TMB with H2O2. In the typical reaction, each 20 μL of Fe-CQDs (2 mg/mL), TMB (5 mM in DMSO) and 10% H2O2 is mixed with 940 μL acetate buffer (0.2 M at pH 4). After 10 min, the absorption spectra of 300–800 nm were monitored by UV–vis spectrometer. The effects of different reaction conditions including concentration of catalyst, pH, time and temperature on the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CQDs were explored by the same procedures.

Subsequently, 0.1 mM of TMB was mixed with various concentrations of H2O2 (0.1–10 mM),and 10 mM of H2O2 was mixed with various concentrations of TMB (0.01–0.3 mM) for kinetic testing, The UV–vis absorbance spectra was recorded, and the two kinetic parameters of K
m and V
max , were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application for H2O2 and ascorbic acid detection
ce:section.ce:para: For H2O2 detection, 20 μL of 2 mg/mL Fe-CQDs + 20 μL of 0.3 mM TMB + 940 μL of acetate buffer (0.2 M, pH 4) were mixed well with 20 μL of H2O2 at various concentrations (0–0.6 mM). After 10 min of reaction, absorbance values in the wavelength range of 550–750 nm were collected and a standard curve of H2O2 was plotted. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by the linear calibration plot using the formula 3σ/k with S/N ≤ 3.

For AA detection, 20 μL of 2 mg/mL Fe-CQDs + 20 μL of 1.0 mM H2O2 + 920 μL of acetate buffer (0.2 M, pH 4) were mixed with 20 μL of different concentrations (0–150 μM) of AA and then 20 μL of 0.3 mM TMB was added rapidly. The absorbance values were collected in the wavelength range of 550–750 nm. Similarly, the interference test was carried out with metal ions (K+, Na+, NH4 +, Zn2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+) and reducing agents (Glc, D-fructose, Gly, CA, SA, GSH, L-cys, DL-malic acid) instead of AA.

The AA detection in the roots, stems, and leaves of sweet potato plants (collected from the local countryside) was tested and verified by iodometry. After cleaning and drying the surface moisture, the edible parts of the roots, stem and leaves were ground into a homogenate using a grinder with 2% metaphosphoric acid and 2% EDTA. The obtained tissue homogenate was centrifuged at 1000 r/min for 15 min, and the supernatant was collected and filtered through 0.22 μM microporous membrane to obtain the filtrate. The AA concentration of the diluted filtrate was measured by the above method.

Fe-CQDs were fabricated by simple pyrolysis of PMS precursor. In the process of a high-temperature oil bath (200℃, 12 h), the PMS is cracked and transformed into ultra-small carbon quantum dots. The morphology and crystal structure of Fe-CQDs are investigated by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1a, Fe-CQDs are uniformly dispersed and nearly spherical. Their particle size distribution ranges from 0.50 to 2.70 nm with an ultra-small average particle size of 1.70 nm (Fig. 1c). The HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) shows that most of the Fe-CQDs are amorphous, while a small part of nanodots are crystalline with a lattice fringe of 0.241 nm, which is corresponding to the [100] plane of graphene [36]. The Hyd-CQDs synthesized by direct hydrothermal pyrolysis show the similar morphology and lattice distribution to Fe-CQDs (Fig. S1a, S1b and S1c). Furthermore, the crystal structures of CQDs are studied by XRD. The wide peaks of the two CQDs at approximately 2θ = 24.0°, which corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite in Fig. 1d and Fig. S1d, indicate that most of the CQDs are amorphous and graphitized. The difference of crystal types in CQDs may be related to the transformation of various complex organic compounds in PMS into CQDs with different crystal types [37]. As known from the atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) result, the Fe content of the synthesized Fe-CQDs is 1.57 wt %, which can confirm the successful doping of Fe atoms into CQDs.

FT-IR is used to analyze the changes of surface functional groups during the formation of Fe-CQDs and Hyd-CQDs (Fig. 1e). The broad absorption of CQDs from 3200 to 3400 cm-1 and the peak at 587 cm-1 can be attributed to the presence of -OH/-NH2 and the stretching vibration of C-CS, respectively [37,38]. The new absorption peaks of Fe-CQDs and Hyd-CQDs at 2935 cm-1 are attributed to C-H stretching vibration [25,32,39]. Besides, the occurrence of the positions at 1650 cm-1 and 1404 cm-1 might suggest vibration band characteristics of the -COO- group in the Fe-CQDs and Hyd-CQDs [40,41]. Therefore, FT-IR results have proved that Fe-CQDs and Hyd-CQDs have abundant hydrophilic groups (-COOH/-OH/-NH2) on the surface. The -COOH has been proved to be the key group for substrate capture, which can improve the catalytic activity of the carbon dots [24]. Notably, the -C-N-C-stretching vibration of Fe-CQDs surface (at about 1122 cm-1) shows a blue shift of about 55 cm-1, which may arise from differences in elemental reorganization during oil bath and hydrothermal pyrolysis [29,42]. Comparing the Raman spectra of Fe-CQDs and Hyd-CQDs as shown in Fig. 1f, the CQDs are all found with a weak peak of 650 cm-1, which may be attributed to the different modes of Fe [32]. Both Fe-CQDs and Hyd-CQDs show two characteristic peaks of the D-bands (near 1325 cm-1) and G-bands (near 1550 cm-1). Typically, the integral intensity ratio (I
D
/I
G ) in D-band and G-band indicates the graphene defect degree in the material [43,44]. The I
D
/I
G value of Fe-CQDs and Hyd-CQDs were calculated to be 0.96 and 1.11 respectively. It indicates that CQDs derived from PMS by high heat treatment at 200 °C for 12 h have rich graphene structural defects which have been reported to benefit faster electron transfer rates [45]. In addition, the doping of autogenous Fe PMS may further improve the stability of the catalytic active site in combination with the defects, thus enhancing the catalytic efficiency [29,46].

The C, N, O and Fe elements can be found in the XPS spectra of Fe-CQDs ( Fig. 2a), Hyd-CQDs (Fig. 2b) and PMS (Fig. 2c). Compared to PMS and Hyd-CQDs, Fe-CQDs have a significantly higher content of N and O and a significantly lower content of C, suggesting that oil bath pyrolysis involves not only the cracking of the carbon skeleton, but also the oxidation of surface groups. As depicted in the high solution spectrum of C1s of Fig. 2d, the two peaks at 284.25 eV and 284.75 eV can be classified as C-C/C-N and CC respectively, which are related to the graphite structure and amorphous structure [37]. Besides, the other three peaks of C-S at 283.79 eV, C-O-C at 285.72 eV and CO at 287.69 eV are observed. Encouragingly, compared to the Hyd-CQDs (Fig. S2b) and PMS (Fig. S2e), the spectra of N1s (Fig. 2e) of Fe-CQDs not only show the three main high resolution peaks assigned to pyridinic-N (398.98 eV), pyrrolic-N (399.43 eV) and graphite-N (401.15 eV), but also have a new peak of pyrrolic-like-N at 400.1 eV, indicating a change in the coordination mode of the rich N kernel of Fe-CQDs [47]. The lower proportion of pyridinic-N species and the higher content of Fe in Fe-CQDs probably indicate that numerous pyridinic N-carbon bonds can be selectively cleaved to expose Fe-Nx sites, and the observed peak of 399.43 eV in Fig. 2e may also be attributed to Fe-Nx [48,49]. The high-resolution Fe2p spectra of Fe-CQDs (Fig. 2f) show three peaks at 709.76, 713.69 and 725.72 eV, which are related to Fe2+ 2p 3/2, Fe3+ 2p 3/2 and Fe3+ 2p 1/2, respectively [19,29,32,50]. Among them, Fe 2p 3/2 is divided into two peaks due to the different chemical shifts of Fe3+ and Fe2+ [51]. In addition, the Fe2p spectra of Fe-CQDs are slightly stronger than those of Hyd-CQDs and PMS (Fig. S2c and S2f), which may be attributed to the immobilization of Fe atoms with heteroatoms under the oil bath pyrolysis process [52,53]. Fig. S3 shows the high resolution XPS spectra of O 1 s and S2p, indicating that O and S were successfully doped into Fe-CQDs and Hyd-CQDs, and the O is allocated to more hydrophilic groups such as CO and O-H, which is consistent with the FT-IR results. The oil bath pyrolysis process also causes the local chemical environment changes of S elements in CQDs (Fig. S3d, S4e and S4f). The above morphology and chemical structure data strongly indicated that Fe-CQDs are successfully synthesized via oil bath pyrolysis. The Fe-CQDs have a higher degree of heteroatom doping and defective graphene structures.

To verify the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CQDs, the absorbance in the range of 300–800 nm was monitored with TMB as the substrate·H2O2 oxidizes TMB with a certain catalyst to form a new chromophore, which can result in a new absorption peak at 652 nm. The stronger absorption band at 652 nm and much darker blue color of Fe-CQDs and Hyd-CQDs can be observed in Fig. 3a, which indicates that PMS is an excellent source for the synthesis of POD-like. Further, the higher catalytic activity of Fe-CQDs suggests that the oil bath pyrolysis is more efficient than hydrothermal method for the synthesis of iron-based nanoenzymes from PMS.

The catalytic activity of Fe-CQDs can be affected by the concentration of catalyst, pH, reaction temperature and reaction time. As shown in Fig. 3d, the absorbance at 652 nm increased with catalyst concentration and shows maximum absorbance at 70 μg/mL. Besides, the maximum absorptions of the reaction system are observed at pH 4 (Fig. S4a),and temperature of 35°C (Fig. S4b) for 40 min reaction time (Fig. S4c). When the pH is lower than 4, the TMB tends to be converted to yellow biphenylquinone which is in the peroxide state. When the pH is higher than 4, H2O2 tends to decompose into H2O and O2 leading to a decrease of H2O2 in the reaction solution [43]. Fe-CQDs maintain good catalytic activity within 20–35°C. However, when the temperature is higher than 35°C, the H2O2 of the system decomposes thus leading to a decrease in the catalytic rate. Considering the catalytic efficiency, economy and ease of operation, the reaction system is carried out at room temperature with a catalyst concentration of 40 μg/mL and an acetate buffer of pH 4.

To provide a detailed explanation of the peroxidase-mimic activity of Fe-CQDs, the concentration of one substrate was fixed in order to explore its corresponding kinetic parameters. According to Beer-Lambert's law, the initial reaction rate V is derived from the calculated slope of TMB concentration over time. When the concentration of TMB is fixed at 0.1 mM and the concentration of H2O2 is from 0.1 to 8.0 mM, the initial speed of the reaction within a certain time is recorded as shown in Fig. 3b. It is found that the reaction catalyzed by Fe-CQDs conforms to the Mchaelis-Menten low (Eq. 1). Furthermore, the kinetic constant K
m and the maximum reaction velocity (V
max ) are further acquired by Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plots (Fig. 3c). Where V
max is maximum velocity, K
m is Michaelis-Menton constant, V is initial velocity, [S] is the substrate concentration.

The kinetic constant K
m of enzyme indicates the affinity between substrate and enzyme, and the smaller the K
m value, the stronger the affinity is. Conversely, we recorded reactions with fixed H2O2 concentration of 10 mM and TMB concentrations ranging from 0.01 to 0.3 mM, as described above (Fig. 3e and f). In Table 1, Fe-CQDs show a better affinity for both TMB and H2O2 than the natural enzyme (HRP), indicating the high affinity and catalytic efficiency of Fe-CQDs as peroxidase-like nanozyme. Strikingly, although the V
max and K
m (TMB) values of Fe-CQDs are not significantly superior to the other metal-based catalysts listed in Table 1, the
K
m
(
H
2
O
2
)
values of Fe-CQDs is significantly lower, demonstrating the high affinity of Fe-CQDs for H2O2. The maximum reaction rate can be obtained at a lower H2O2 concentration, which is probably benefiting from the binding sites of the rich carboxyl groups on the surface of Fe-CQDs.

The above results confirmed that Fe-CQDs have a high peroxide-like activity, and their catalytic oxidation activity towards TMB depends on the concentration of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 4a, the absorbance at 652 nm increases gradually with an increase in H2O2 concentration. The discoloration process of the system from colorless to blue well displayed a H2O2-dependent behavior. Good detection linearity (Fig. 4d) in the linear range of H2O2 0.5–40 μM (R2 =0.9975) can be achieved, and the calculated LOD of (3σ/k) is 0.147 μM. Compared with the other literatures listed in Table S1, a lower detection limit can be acquired with Fe-CQDs. This is probably related to the high affinity of Fe-CQDs to H2O2, suggesting its quantitative potential in trace H2O2 systems.

AA is found in abundance in fresh fruits, and its content is used to assess the freshness and nutritional quality of those fruits. Therefore, a method with a wide detection range is particularly critical to deal with different content distribution of samples or a low enough detection limits to meet the detection of ultra-low concentration samples. Herein, an AA colorimetric sensing platform is developed based on the fading of oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) due to the high reducibility of AA. As shown in Fig. 4b, the absorbance of ox-TMB at 652 nm decreases gradually and the color of the solution changes from blue to transparent with the increase of AA concentration. A good linear relationship (y = 0.00443x+0.01908 (R2 =0.9806)) with a range from 1 to 120 μM between the absorbance at 652 nm and the concentration of AA is obtained (Fig. 4e). The LOD is calculated to be as low as 0.435 μM (3σ/k). This LOD is significantly superior to most of peroxidase mimics detection systems or has a wider detection range compared to the reported materials in Table 2, which also manifests that our work has broad application prospects for AA detection in food analysis, biosensing and clinical fields.

According to previous reports, iron-based peroxidase mimics mainly depend on Fenton/Fenton-like reactions with H2O2 to promote the production of intermediates •OH [21,50]. To evaluate the production of •OH during the catalytic process, isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and methanol (MA) are employed as hydroxyl radical trapping agents to monitor the presence of •OH. As shown in Fig. 4c, the absorbance of ox-TMB at 652 nm is dependent on the concentration of IPA and MA, which gradually decreases with the increasing concentration of IPA and MA, and IPA has stronger obstruction to catalysis. These results greatly confirmed that •OH, as an active intermediate in the oxidation process, promotes the transfer of its electrons to the surface of TMB for oxidation [63,64]. Additionally, benzoquinone (BQ) almost, has no effect on the catalytic effect even at a higher concentration, which excludes the involvement of •O2 - in TMB catalytic oxidation. These results further indicate the mechanism in Fig. 4f. The Fe-CQDs can work as peroxidase mimics mainly attributed to its ability to mediate H2O2 to produce and transfer •OH to TMB surface.

Common mineral ions and reducing agents were chosen to evaluate the selectivity of the AA detection method. As shown in Fig. S5a, with (Red Bar) and without (Black Bar) AA, the effect of metal ions except for Fe2+ and Fe3+on TMB is negligible (Abs response <±8%). The slight promoting effect of Fe2+/Fe3+ on TMB oxidation may be attributed to their redox properties. Some metal ion masking agents such as EDTA are recommended to be added during the extraction process.In Fig.S5(b), CA, L-cys and DL-malic acid have been found to reduce ox-TMB to a certain extent due to the α-hydroxyl groups of CA and DL-malic acid [25] and the -SH group of L-cys Encouragingly, the interference from the three organic acids to the AA detection are not obvious when they coexist with AA. This can be attributed to the stronger reductive property of the enediol group of AA which makes AA much more easily oxidized by ox-TMB than CA, L-cys and DL-malic acid [65–67]. Good stability is essential for an excellent nanozyme in practical application. As shown in Fig. S6a, Fe-CQDs exhibit stable absorption characteristics even in NaCl solutions up to 500 mM. Similarly, the absorption changes of Fe-CQDs in acidic medium (pH 2–6) and neutral to alkaline medium (pH 7–12) are negligible (Fig. S6b). Furthermore, the absorption properties of Fe-CQDs show no significantly changes after 60 min of irradiation under normal light conditions(Fig. S6c). Therefore, Fe-CQDs synthesized by the oil bath exhibit high optical stability and long durability.

Sweet potatoes are known to be one of nature's most richest vegetables in terms of AA [68,69]. Many studies have focused on the AA content in sweet potato roots to confirm the mature. Mature sweet potato roots contain approximately 70–150 mg/100 g of AA in different regions [70,71]. Herein, Fe-CQDs were applied to the whole sweet potato plant, including root, stem and leaf to explore the reliability of the established AA colorimetric sensor for practical application. The iodometry as the reference method was used to verify the results.The contents, recovery rates and relative standard deviations (RSDs) of AA determined with the two methods are shown in Table S2. As known from the results, the AA colorimetric sensor has ideal accuracy. Besides, the AA content in the three different parts of the sweet potato plant significantly differed, The leaf has the higher AA content than, the root and the stem. The recoveries of AA are 100.43–105.77% in roots, 96.15–105.92 % in stems and 96.39–103.99% in leaves, respectively. The RSDs for the three repeated tests are all less than 3%. Therefore, Fe-CQDs can be as an effective peroxidase-like nanozyme for the determination of the AA content in actual samples.

In general, we have fabricated Hyd-CQDs and Fe-CQDs with PMS by hydrothermal and oil bath thermal strategies. Fe-CQDs have surface carboxyl groups and abundant carbon-deficient structure, and exhibit higher peroxidase-like activity and stability. Fe-CQDs can be as peroxidase-like nanozyme to accelerate the oxidation of TMB by H2O2.·OH acted as the main active intermediate in this oxidation process. Based on the affinity of Fe-CQDs for H2O2 and the reduction of Fe-CQDs/H2O2/TMB system by AA, a sensitive colorimetric platform for H2O2 and AA detection with a low LOD of 0.147 μM and 0.435 μM respcetively was achieved. Furthermore, this colorimetric platform was successfully applied in the AA quantification of roots, stems and leaves of sweet potatoes with an ideal recovery rate. Our work not only demonstrates the development potential of PMS in the field of nanomaterials, but also further provides a new application for the high value utilization of PMS.

Xiaotong Huang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Mingxia Jiang: Formal analysis, Visualization, Investigation. Hui Zeng: Visualization, Investigation. Junxian Wu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiaxin Wu: Investigation. Xiaojing Liu: Investigation. Lihua Zhou: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yong Yuan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
